coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive and selective fluorimetric probe for intracellular peroxynitrite based on photoinduced electron transfer from ferrocene to carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jiali
Sun, Shan
Jiang, Kai
Wang, Yuhui
Liu, Wenqing
Lin, Hengwei
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a highly sensitive and selective fluorimetric nanoprobe for peroxynitrite (ONOO−) detection based on photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from ferrocene (Fc) to carbon dots (CDs) is reported. The nanoprobe (named CDs-Fc) can be facilely constructed through covalently conjugating CDs and ferrocenecarboxylic acid. Further studies reveal that the energy level of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the CDs is lowered with the addition of ONOO− due to its oxidation and nitration capabilities. Thus, an efficient electron transfer from Fc to the excited states of CDs could occur, leading to obvious fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe was determined to be peroxynitrite concentrations dependence with a linear range between 4nM to 0.12μM. Thanks to the excellent optical properties of the CDs and efficient electron transfer efficiency from Fc to the excited CDs, the nanoprobe exhibits very high sensitivity to ONOO− with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.9nM. To the best of our knowledge, this LOD is the highest reported value till today for the detection of peroxynitrite. Besides, the nanoprobe also shows excellent selectivity to ONOO− among a broad range of substances, even including other reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (ROS/RNS). Finally, the nanoprobe was verified to be very low cytotoxicity, and was successfully applied for intracellular ONOO− detection. This work would provide a promising tool for the research of ONOO− in cytobiology and disease diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ferrocene
Photoinduced electron transfer (PET)
Peroxynitrite
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and principle of the PET nanoprobe for ONOO−
Preparation and characterizations of the CDs
Preparation and characterization of the nanoprobe (CDs-Fc)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of the CDs (black line) and CDs-Fc (red line). (b) TEM image of the CDs-Fc. (c) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs-Fc in PB buffer (0.1M, pH 7.4) under different excitation wavelengths. (d) Fluorescence excitation (λem = 640nm) and emission spectra (λex = 540nm) of CDs (1μg/mL) and CDs-Fc (1μg/mL), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe (1μg/mL) in the presence of different concentrations of ONOO−. (b) Fitting curve between fluorescence quenching efficiency and concentration of ONOO−. (c) Linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and ONOO− concentration range from 4 to 120nM. All experiments were performed in PB buffer (pH 7.4, 0.1M). The error bars in (b) and (c) represent three independent measurements.

Relative fluorescence response (F0-F/F0) of the nanoprobe (1μg/mL) in the presence of different inspected species. F0: FL intensity of the blank, F: FL intensity of the nonoprobe upon the addition of inspected interferences. ONOO−: 0.2μM, RNS/ROS: 2μM, other substances: 0.1mM.

Confocal fluorescence imaging of MCF-7 cells. (a) Fluorescence image of the cells incubated with the nanoprobe (denoted as the control, 50μg/mL), (b) and (c) are the corresponding bright field and merged images. (d) MCF-7 Cells were firstly incubated with the nanoprobe (50μg/mL) and then treated with LPS (1µg/mL) and IFN-γ (100ng/mL), and (e) and (f) are the corresponding bright field and merged images. Excitation wavelength: 552nm. The fluorescence emission is collected from 555 to 780nm. Scale bar: 25µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic Illustration of the preparation and principle of the ONOO− Nanoprobe Based on PET from Fc to the excited CDs.
(a) The energy levels of HOMO and LUMO of CDs, CDs treatment with ONOO−, and Fc, respectively, and the possible electron transfer processes. (b) Supposed reactions of ONOO− with the CDs.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive and selective fluorimetric probe for intracellular peroxynitrite based on photoinduced electron transfer from ferrocene to carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Functional Materials and Nano-Devices, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Functional Materials and Nano-Devices, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a highly sensitive and selective fluorimetric nanoprobe for peroxynitrite (ONOO−) detection based on photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from ferrocene (Fc) to carbon dots (CDs) is reported. The nanoprobe (named CDs-Fc) can be facilely constructed through covalently conjugating CDs and ferrocenecarboxylic acid. Further studies reveal that the energy level of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the CDs is lowered with the addition of ONOO− due to its oxidation and nitration capabilities. Thus, an efficient electron transfer from Fc to the excited states of CDs could occur, leading to obvious fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe was determined to be peroxynitrite concentrations dependence with a linear range between 4nM to 0.12μM. Thanks to the excellent optical properties of the CDs and efficient electron transfer efficiency from Fc to the excited CDs, the nanoprobe exhibits very high sensitivity to ONOO− with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.9nM. To the best of our knowledge, this LOD is the highest reported value till today for the detection of peroxynitrite. Besides, the nanoprobe also shows excellent selectivity to ONOO− among a broad range of substances, even including other reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (ROS/RNS). Finally, the nanoprobe was verified to be very low cytotoxicity, and was successfully applied for intracellular ONOO− detection. This work would provide a promising tool for the research of ONOO− in cytobiology and disease diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Peroxynitrite (ONOO−), a kind of reactive nitrogen species (RNS) that generating from nitric oxide free radical and superoxide anion in vivo, plays significant roles in many physiological and pathological processes (Radi, 2013; Ferrer-Sueta and Radi, 2009). Due to possessing strong oxidation and nitration capabilities, peroxynitrite has been shown to damage a great deal of molecules in cells, such as DNA and proteins. In addition, peroxynitrite also plays positive roles on signal transduction, immune response, redox homeostasis, and so on (Alvarez and Radi, 2003; Pacher et al., 2007; Shanlin et al., 1997). The abnormal levels of peroxynitrite indicate the appearances of inflammation, cardiovascular disease, multiple sclerosis and neurodegenerative disorders (Zhou et al., 2015). However, due to the short life span and low steady-state concentration, ONOO− is difficultly to be detected, especially in biological environment. Therefore, the exploitation of sensitive and selective techniques for peroxynitrite sensing is highly desirable and meaningful.

Up to now, many technologies including electron spin resonance (EPR) (Dikalov et al., 1997), chromatography (Frankenfeld et al., 2006), UV–visible absorption spectrometry (Bedioui et al., 2011), and electrochemistry (Alvarez et al., 1996) have been developed to monitor the concentration variations of ONOO− in biosystems. Among these methods, spectrofluorimetry represents a powerful tool and has been paid increasing attention due to its fascinating merits (e.g. high sensitivity, noninvasive, and high imaging contrast). Among the reported fluorescent probes for ONOO−, organic dyes-based probes are the most cases to be synthesized and researched (Zhang et al., 2012, 2015; Song et al., 2010; Peng et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2013). For example, Zhang et al. developed a mitochondria-targetable fluorescent probe for ONOO− sensing (Zhang et al., 2015). Song et al. (2010) exploited a lanthanide-complex-based ratiometric probe for specific ONOO− detection in living cells (Song et al., 2010). However, these organic dye-based fluorescent probes have some native drawbacks, such as complicated synthesis procedures, low photostability and/or poor water solubility (Tian et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2006). Besides, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have also attracted extensive attention in sensing, bioimaging, optoelectronics and nanomedicine (Zheng et al., 2015; Freeman and Willner, 2012). Specifically, Adegoke et al. developed a highly sensitive nanoprobe for ONOO− detection with the utilization of thiol-capped QDs (Adegoke and Nyokong, 2013). Nonetheless, the commonly high toxicity of heavy metal ions in QDs should be taken into consideration. So, it is significant and interesting to develop new types of fluorimetric probe for ONOO− sensing.

As a newly discovered inorganic phosphor, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted broad attentions in recent years (Lim et al., 2015; Wang and Hu, 2014; Pan et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2015). Compared to organic dyes and QDs, CDs hold many advantages including easy preparation, low toxicity, and high chemical stability (Wang and Hu, 2014; Pan et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2015). Especially, the superior biocompatibility and non-photobleaching of CDs makes them highly appropriate in biologically relevant applications (e.g. sensing and imaging in cell, tissue, or even in vivo) (Ding et al., 2014; Loo et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2015; Hsu et al., 2013). More recently, highly fluorescent CDs with heteroatom doping of phosphorus and nitrogen have been synthesized and exploited for ONOO− detection (Gong et al., 2016; Simões et al., 2015). Unfortunately, these CDs-based probes are poor in selectivity and/or sensitivity due to lack of necessary surface functionalization, thus being unsuitable for intracellular sensing applications.

It has been demonstrated that CDs are excellent electron donors or acceptors in many previous studies, which indicates that they are ideal and promising candidates for the construction of PET-based probes (Chai et al., 2015). Ferrocene (Fc) has been widely employed in electrochemistry due to its good chemical stability and excellent electrochemical property (Feng et al., 2016a; Feng et al., 2016b). More recently, Fc had been used to design PET-based organic molecular probes as an efficient electron donor (Martínez et al., 2006; Zhang et al., 2008; Kaur et al., 2016). Inspired by these knowledge, herein, we designed and constructed a novel PET nanoprobe based on Fc modified CDs for the first time. This nanoprobe is shown to be highly sensitive and selective towards ONOO− in both aqueous buffer and biological samples. More importantly, the nanoprobe is further demonstrated to be suitable for ONOO− sensing in living cells, thanks to its low cytotoxicity, high detection sensitivity and selectivity.

Methanol, dichloromethane, formamide, KCl, NaCl, Na2HPO4·12H2O, KH2PO4, MgSO4, NaOH, NaNO2, Ca(NO3)2, glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), trypsin, pepsin, 1-hydroxybenzotriazole (HOBT), and N, N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, ferrocenecarboxylic acid, glycine, L-cysteine, glutathione (GSH), KO2, MnO2, H2O2, NaNO3, NaClO, sodium nitroprusside (SNP) were provided from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Lipopolysaccharides (LPS) from Escherichia coli 0111:B4 was from Alfa Aesar. Interferon-γ (INF-γ) was obtained from Beijing T&L Biotech Co., Ltd. Fe(ClO4)2, Fe(ClO4)3, Zn(ClO4)2 were obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized (DI) water.

Microwave chemical reactor (MDS-6G, Shanghai SINEO Microwave Chemistry Technology) was used to synthesize carbon dots. The sizes and morphologies of CDs and CDs-Fc were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai F20) operated at 200kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer using the KBr pellet technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an AXIS ULTRA DLD spectrograph with Al/Kα as the source. The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer and a PERSEE T10CS UV–Vis spectrophotometer, respectively. Fluorescence lifetimes were tested using Fluorolog 3–11 (HORIBA Jobin Yvon). The absolute fluorescence quantum yields of CDs and CDs-Fc were determined by a Fluoromax-4 measurement system (HORIBA, JobinYvon. Inc). Cell imaging of the nanoprobe was performed on a confocal laser fluorescence microscopy (TCS SP5II, Leica, Germany). The oxidation-reduction potentials of CDs, CDs-Fc, and Fc were measured by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) with a standard three-electrode system (glassy carbon as working electrode, freshly cleaned Pt wire as counter electrode, and Ag wire as quasi-reference electrode). The test samples were dissolved in N, N′-dimethyl formamide (DMF) containing 0.1M supporting electrolyte (tetra-n-butylammonium hexafluorohphosphate). In order to obtain absolute voltage, bis-(pentamethylcyclopentadieny) iron (II) was used as an internal standard. All of the electrochemical tests were performed on a model CHI832C electrochemical work station (CH Instruments, Shanghai).

The CDs were synthesized by a microwave-assisted solvothermal route according to our previous report (Sun et al., 2016). Briefly, 5% (weight) citric acid dissolved in formamide was subjected to a microwave reaction (160°C for one hour and 120°C for another one hour). After cooling the autoclave to room temperature naturally, a crude product was harvested through precipitation upon the introduction of acetone (80%, v/v) at −20°C overnight. Then, the precipitation was washed by acetone and followed by methanol/acetone (1/10, v/v) three times, respectively. Thereafter, the precipitation was re-dissolved in methanol, collected the supernatant solution and filtrated by 0.22µm millipore membrane. Finally, the CDs was obtained by evaporating methanol and further dried under vacuum.

The CDs had been confirmed holding abundant amino functional groups on their surface and thus could be facilely covalently conjugated to Fc-COOH by the standard DCC protocol (Mao et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2014). In brief, Fc-COOH (15.6mg, 0.068mmol) was dissolved in 15mL ethanol, and then HOBT (11.5mg, 0.075mmol) and DCC (14.0mg, 0.068mmol) were added to the solution to activate the carboxyl groups. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1h. Then, the CDs (15mg) were added into the above solution and stirred for 24h. After evaporating off solvent, a black product was harvested. Finally, the product was purified by column chromatography using dichloromethane/methanol as the eluent.

For the detection of ONOO−, various concentrations of ONOO − were added into the nanoprobe (1μg/mL) dissolved in phosphate buffer (PB) (100mM, pH 7.4). And then, fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer under ambient condition. Furthermore, the feasibility of the nanoprobe for ONOO− detection in complex biological samples was investigated, and a 50-fold diluted bovine serum was selected as an example. And then the identical detection procedure as that of in aqueous buffer was followed for ONOO−. To evaluate the specificity of the nanoprobe, other ROS/RNS (types and preparation procedures being provided in Supporting Information (SI)), biomolecules and ions were added into the solution of nanoprobe in place of ONOO − with identical experimental conditions. The emission intensities at 640nm were monitored for quantitative analysis.

A Standard MTT assay against MCF-7 cells was adopted to examine the cytotoxicity of the nanoprobe. In brief, MCF-7 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate (1 × 105 cells per mL) and adhered overnight. After a cultivation of 24h at 37°C, cells were then treated with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) containing various amount of the nanoprobe (10–50μg/mL) for another 24h incubation. Thereafter, the culture medium was removed and 10µL MTT (5.0mg/mL in PBS) was added into each well for additional 4h incubation. Following the discard of the growth medium, 100µL of DMSO was added to dissolve MTT. Finally, the optical absorption intensity of each sample was recorded by a microplate reader.

The potential application of the nanoprobe for intracellular ONOO− sensing was studied in MCF-7 cells. In brief, 2.0mL of MCF-7 cells in DMEM at an initial density of 4 × 104 cell per mL were seeded in each dish and cultured at 37°C for 24h in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The nanoprobe (50μg/mL) diluted with DMEM was incubated with MCF-7 cells for another 6h, and then washed three times with PBS buffer to remove the free CDs. Subsequently, interferon-γ (IFN-γ, 0.1μg/mL) and lipopolysaccharide (LPS, 1μg/mL) were added and the cells and were incubated for 6h. Finally, fluorescence imaging experiments were carried by confocal laser fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and principle of the PET nanoprobe for ONOO−
ce:section.ce:para: The ONOO− nanoprobe is built on the basis of electron transfer from Fc to the excited CDs. Typically, Fc as an electron donor has been applied for the design of many sensor due to their superior electrochemical characters. Recent reports demonstrated that Fc has also been adopted as a promising electron acceptor in some fluorescent dye-based highly efficient PET probe (Martínez et al., 2006; Zhang et al., 2008; Kaur et al., 2016). In addition, photoexcited CDs are excellent electron donors or electron acceptors, which has been described in many previous works (Shao et al., 2016; Bui and Park, 2016; He et al., 2016; Russ Algar et al., 2014). Encouraged by this information, as shown in Scheme 1, Fc is selected and covalently modified on the surface of CDs through classical DCC conjugation strategy and further designed as a nanoprobe for ONOO− sensing. In the presence of ONOO−, CDs tend to be nitrated. As a result, the HOMO level of CDs is lowered and efficient electron transfer would occur from Fc to the excited CDs through a PET process, thus inducing CDs’ fluorescence quenching greatly. Owing to the excellent optical properties of CDs, the nanoprobe shows high sensitivity towards ONOO−. Importantly, the good biocompatibility of CDs makes this nanoprobe to be suitable for ONOO− detection in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the above design, the CDs were first prepared and characterized according to our previous report (Sun et al., 2016). As seen in Fig. S1a and S1b (Supplementary information), the as-prepared CDs possess uniform morphologies, narrow size distribution (average diameter ca. 3.5nm) and good monodispersity. Subsequently, the photophysical properties of CDs were studied by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrometer. In addition, the CDs were determined covering abundant amino functional groups on their surface from their FT-IR characterization ( Fig. 1a). Fig. S1c shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs under different excitation wavelengths, which represents the optimum emission (640nm) nearly constant with the excitation wavelengths varying from 480 to 600nm. Such an excitation-independent fluorescence emission property has been found in many previous reports and is usually attributed to the surface states/defects induced emission. Fig. S1d represents the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs. The CDs show wide absorption from 300nm to 600nm with typical peaks at 350nm and 540nm, which are generally attributed to the π−π* and n−π* transitions of aromatic π systems containing C–N/C˭N or C˭O bond, respectively. The fluorescent excitation spectrum indicates that the optimum excitation wavelength for the CDs is at 540nm. Moreover, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs is measured to be 21.2%, which is competitive to the reported organic fluorescent dyes and inorganic phosphors (Wang et al., 2014a; Kawasaki et al., 2011). In addition, the effects of pH on the emission of CDs were studied. As seen in Fig. S1e, the CDs show stable emission at pH ranging from 4 to 8 even under 100mM NaCl ionic strength. There superior optical properties make the CDs to be promising candidates for the construction of nanoprobes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the nanoprobe (CDs-Fc)
ce:section.ce:para: Due to covering abundant amino groups on the surface of CDs, the nanoprobe (i.e. CDs-Fc) can thus be facilely prepared via a coupling reaction between CDs and ferrocenecarboxylic acid. To verify the successful conjugation, FT-IR spectra measurement was carried out. As shown in Fig. 1a, the typical absorption peaks at 1400, 1650 and 3350cm−1 demonstrate the formation of amide functional groups, accompanying by the peak intensity decrease of −NH2 at 3200cm−1. Additionally, the absorption at 660cm−1 is a unique bending vibration of C˭C of cyclopentadiene on Fc. To further confirm the successful modification, XPS and UV–Vis absorption characterizations of CDs-Fc were performed. As shown in Fig. S2, typical peak of Fe (2p) at 707.7eV is observed. In Fig. S3, the absorption spectrum of CDs-Fc shows both typical peak characteristics of CDs and Fc. These analyses clearly illustrate the successful linking between CDs and Fc. The TEM image in Fig. 1b shows that the size and morphology of CDs-Fc do not alter after the Fc modification. In Figs. 1c and 1d, it is easily to find that the fluorescence spectra of CDs show only slight change after their conjugations with Fc. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime of CDs-Fc were determined to be 19.5% and 1.54ns (Fig. S4b) after the conjugation of Fc, which are similar as that of the free CDs (21.2% and 1.60ns (Fig. S4a)). These results demonstrate that the modification of Fc shows negligible effects on the fluorescence properties of the CDs. Furthermore, the photostability of the nanoprobe was also studied. As shown in Fig. S5–S7, the nanoprobe holds excellent photostability under continuous ultraviolet light irradiation, relatively stable emission at pH values between 4 to 8, and good tolerance to high ionic strengths up to 500mM of NaCl. Such a stable fluorescence emission makes the nanoprobe to be robust for applications in sensing.

For ONOO− sensing, incremental amounts of ONOO− are sequentially added into the nanoprobe (1μg/mL). As seen in Fig. 2a, fluorescence of the nanoprobe gradually decreases with continuous addition of ONOO− from 0.05nM to 0.4μM, and a platform is then reached with higher concentrations (Fig. 2b). The maximum quenching efficiency is calculated to be ca. 80%. Moreover, the fluorescence decrease of the nanoprobe is found linearly relevant to ONOO− concentrations from 4nM to 0.12μM with good linear correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.993) (Fig. 2c). Consequently, the detection limit for ONOO− is calculated to be 2.9nM according to the standard 3σ (signal-to-noise) criteria (Garranzo-Asensio et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2013). In order to demonstrate the practicability of this nanoprobe, a peroxynitrite titration experiment was further carried out in a complicated biological media (i.e. bovine serum with 50-fold diluted). As seen in Fig. S8, fluorescence emission of the nanoprobe gradually decreases upon the addition of ONOO− from 0 to 10μM, and a standard fitting curve is obtained similarly with that of in buffer solution. These results clearly verify that the nanoprobe shows high sensitivity for ONOO− detection both in aqueous buffer and complicated biological samples.

To determine the mechanism of ONOO− induced fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe, control experiments, fluorescence lifetimes and electrochemical characterizations were followed performed. As seen in Fig. S9, fluorescence intensity of the free CDs (1μg/mL) shows only ca. 20% quenching in the presence of 0.2μM of ONOO−, which is much smaller than that of the nanoprobe (i.e. CDs-Fc) upon addition of the same concentration of ONOO− (see Fig. 2a). This result demonstrates that the modification of Fc on the surface of CDs promotes fluorescence quenching by ONOO− significantly. We speculate that this fluorescence quenching may be attributed to efficient PET process from Fc to the excited CDs, as illustrated in Scheme 2a. To understand such a process, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of CDs, CDs-Fc, CDs after treatment with ONOO− and Fc were obtained through combination of electrochemical measurements and UV−Vis absorption spectra.

In Fig. S10, it is clearly to observe that the CDs and CDs-Fc exhibit similar redox potentials, indicating that the surface modification of Fc having no obvious effect on their chemical characteristics. However, upon the addition of ONOO−, the redox potentials of CDs changes greatly (Fig. S10c), which illustrates that certain reactions have occurred between the CDs and ONOO− (see possible reactions in Scheme 2b). Through comparing the FT-IR spectra of CDs before and after treatment with ONOO− (Fig. S11), one can see unique peaks of –NO2 (1373, 1180, 858, and 947cm−1) and ammonium (1076 and 2100–2700cm−1) (Al-Qadiri, 2011). To further confirm the nitration reaction of CDs, a generally accepted nitrating agent (i.e. HNO2) was selected to treat CDs. As seen in Fig. S12a, fluorescence of the CDs was greatly quenched with the addition of HNO2. The reason for this fluorescence quenching might be attributed to the formation of strong electron-withdrawing functional groups, which can be verified by the FT-IR spectrum measurement of CDs after treating with HNO2. As shown in Fig. S12b, unique peaks of nitrosamine (-NH-N˭O) at 802, 1289 and 1625cm−1 are observed, and meanwhile the peak intensity of −NH2 at 3200cm−1 decreases. These results demonstrate that the surface property of the CDs is changed through the chemical reaction between amine groups and nitrating agent (i.e. HNO2 and ONOO−). Consequently, unique nitration and oxidation reactions are proposed to have occurred upon the addition of ONOO−, which induced the HOMO and LUMO of CDs to change sharply. Typically, the HOMO−LUMO energy gap (Eg) should match with the excitation energy. Therefore, 0.26eV and −1.98eV (vs. saturated calomel electrode (SCE)) are selected as the first oxidation (φ ox) and reduction (φ red) potentials of the CDs, which are corresponding to the HOMO and LUMO energy levels, respectively. Consequently, the absolute energy levels of HOMO and LUMO are calculated to be −5.0eV and −2.76eV, respectively, according to the following empirical formulas (Ma et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2014; Ji et al., 2016):
(1)
E HOMO
eV
= − (
φ ox
+
4. 74 )
(2)
E
LUMO
(
eV
)
= − (
φ
red
+ 4.74 )

Similarly, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of Fc and CDs after treatment with ONOO− can be determined and are illustrated in Scheme 2a. As seen from this Scheme, due to the higher HOMO energy level of CDs than that of Fc (Fig. S10d), the excited electrons of CDs preferentially return to their ground state (HOMO) and give fluorescence emission. However, after the treatment with ONOO−, the HOMO energy level of CDs decreases obviously, and becomes lower than that of Fc. So, the ground state electron of Fc can transfer to the HOMO of the excited CDs via a PET process, preventing return of the excited electron of CDs and leading to a distinct fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of the nanoprobe in the absence (1.54ns) or presence (1.62ns) of ONOO− is similar (Fig. S4), which further supports the steady electron transfer based quenching mechanism (Wu et al., 2017; Yan et al., 2016). Therefore, these results and discussion clearly illustrate that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the CDs with addition of ONOO− should be an efficient PET process from Fc to the excited CDs.

In order to assess the specificity of this nanoprobe, interferences from many biological-related substances including ions (e.g. K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and NO3 −), small molecules (e.g. glucose, GSH, and AA), ROS (e.g·H2O2, •OH, 1O2, and O2•−), and RNS (e.g. NO) were studied. As shown in Fig. 3, no obvious fluorescence alteration is observed even when the concentrations of ROS/RNS are 10-fold higher than that of ONOO−. Furthermore, other inspected substances also cause only tiny fluorescence variation at a concentration of 0.1mM, i.e. 500-fold higher than that of ONOO− (0.2μM). These results clearly demonstrate excellent specificity of this nanoprobe for peroxynitrite detection. In Table S1, a detailed comparison on analytical performance (e.g. limit of detection (LOD), selectivity, and linear range) between recently published probes and this nanoprobe is offered. As seen therein, the as-proposed nanoprobe exhibits a quite competitive sensitivity towards ONOO–, which is mainly attributed to the superior optical characters of CDs and efficient PET process. In addition, the unique nitration and oxidation reactions between CDs and ONOO− are considered to be responsible for the high sensitivity.

Owing to the good analytical performance (high sensitivity and specificity), the nanoprobe is expected for further intracellular ONOO− sensing. In order to be applied in biological samples, the cytotoxicity of the nanoprobe should be taken into consideration, and consequently a standard MTT examination was performed. As seen in Fig. S13, over 90% cell viability is observed after being incubated MCF-7 cells with the nanoprobe (10–50μg/mL) for 24h. The results reveal that the nanoprobe is highly biocompatible, and could be applied for intracellular imaging.

Finally, the proposed nanoprobe is attempted to be applied to monitor the level of intracellular ONOO− by confocal fluorescence microscopy. MCF-7, a type of human mammary cancer cell, is selected as a research model, which can be induced by interferon-γ (IFN-γ) and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) to generate peroxynitrite (Peng et al., 2014; Miao et al., 2016). MCF-7 cells are firstly mixed and incubated with the nanoprobe. Subsequently, the cells are treated with IFN-γ/LPS to stimulate and produce peroxynitrite. As seen in Fig. 4, the nanoprobe shows clear red fluorescence emission in MCF-7 cells, but great quenching after treatment with LPS/IFN-γ. The results demonstrate that the nanoprobe is applicable for visual detection of ONOO− in living cells.

In summary, a nanoprobe that constructed based on Fc modified CDs for the highly specific and sensitive detection of intracellular peroxynitrite is reported. The sensing principle is on the basis of a PET process from Fc to the excited CDs in the presence of ONOO−, which has been verified by their corresponding HOMO and LUMO energy levels. Thanks to the excellent optical properties and efficient PET, the nanoprobe exhibits very high sensitivity. Owing to the unique nitration and oxidation reactions between ONOO− and CDs, the nanoprobe shows very high specificity towards ONOO−. Furthermore, the superior biocompatibility nature of CDs makes the nanoprobe suitable for intracellular ONOO− sensing. This work would open up new opportunities and provide a robust tool for peroxynitrite-related biological research and clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Natural Science Foundation of China (21305152), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LY16B050005) and Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2014A610195, 2014B82010, 2015A610277 and 2016C50009). The authors would also sincerely thank Prof. Bin Su and Dr. Weiliang Guo from the Chemistry Department of Zhejiang University for their assistance on the electrochemical measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.05.054.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Al-Qadiri, H., 2011. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectroscopy. LAP Lambert.
